analysis.note: Demonstrative pronoun; visarga is lost before eva in external sandhi.
The final -a of eva coalesces with the following initial a- of anāsravaḥ; the segmented sandhi form is shown.
Adjective agreeing with dhātuḥ; its initial a- is involved in the preceding vowel coalescence, and final -aḥ becomes -o before dhātuḥ.
Masculine nominative singular head noun of the first nominal predication.
Adjective agreeing with dhātuḥ; final -aḥ becomes -o before voiced dh- in dhruvaḥ.
Adjective in the same nominative series.
Here masculine nominative singular adjective, not neuter noun sukham; final -aḥ becomes -o before v-.
Compound stem treated as a whole, per instruction; final -aḥ becomes -o before asau.

verification.detail: compound stem vimuktikāya as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Demonstrative pronoun; the initial a- is elided after preceding -o and marked by avagraha.
Adjectival compound meaning 'called/named Dharma'; final -aḥ becomes -o before ayam.

verification.detail: compound stem dharmākhya as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Demonstrative pronoun; initial a- is elided after preceding -o and marked by avagraha, while final -m is printed as anusvāra before m-.
Compound stem treated as a whole; mahat appears as mahā- in prior-member position.

verification.detail: compound stem mahāmuni as Pum/Sasthi/Eka: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: Construe the nominative series in the first line as co-referential predicates/modifiers rather than as independent substantives.
options_considered: one nominal predication with masculine nominative agreement
justifications.chosen: An equational nominal sentence with omitted asti: 'that very one is the ... element'.

evidence.quote: saḥ eva anāsravaḥ dhātuḥ acintyaḥ kuśalaḥ dhruvaḥ sukhaḥ
evidence.translation: Except for eva, these forms are masculine nominative singular; Sanskrit commonly omits asti in such nominal predications.

justifications.decision: Take anāsravaḥ adjectivally with dhātuḥ, not as a separate noun.
options_considered: two separate predicates, 'the outflow-free one' and 'the element'

evidence.translation: Both words are masculine nominative singular; MW s.v. anāsrava gives 'free from mundane inclinations', and MW s.v. dhātu gives 'element/constituent part'.

justifications.decision: Do not construe sukhaḥ as neuter substantive 'happiness'; the verse has masculine nominative singular agreement.

evidence.translation: The masculine ending -ḥ aligns sukhaḥ with the surrounding masculine nominatives; MW s.v. sukha includes the mfn. sense 'pleasant, agreeable'.

justifications.decision: Read vimukti as the dependent first member qualifying kāya, rather than as a coordinate or appositional member.
justifications.chosen: vimuktikāyaḥ as a tatpuruṣa, 'body of liberation'.

evidence.translation: A bare nominal first member before a head noun naturally supports a dependent tatpuruṣa construal; MW s.v. vimukti gives 'release, deliverance, liberation'.

justifications.decision: Analyze dharmākhyaḥ as an adjectival naming compound agreeing with the masculine nominative subject.

evidence.translation: dharmākhyaḥ is masculine nominative singular and agrees with the deictic/predicate complex; MW s.v. ākhyā gives 'appellation, name', supporting 'having Dharma as name/called Dharma'.

justifications.decision: Take mahāmuneḥ as genitive singular of the karmadhāraya compound mahāmuni.
justifications.chosen: mahāmuneḥ is a genitive dependent on the preceding body/designation phrase: 'of the Great Sage'.

evidence.translation: muneḥ is genitive singular, and mahā- is the compound prior-member form of mahat; the genitive most naturally marks possession or relation: 'of the Great Sage'.

justifications.decision: Avoid treating asau and ayam as introducing separate subjects.
options_considered: one appositional chain with repeated deictics
two or three separate deictic subjects
justifications.chosen: saḥ, asau, and ayam are appositional masculine singular deictics pointing to the same referent within the verse.

evidence.translation: All three demonstratives/predicates are masculine nominative singular, so grammar permits a single appositional chain.

translation:
That very one is the outflow-free element (anāsrava-dhātu),
inconceivable, wholesome, steadfast, blissful;
that body of liberation (vimuktikāya)—this one of the Great Sage (mahāmuni)—
is called Dharma (dharmākhya).
one_shot_delta: Because this is an ablation run without bhāṣya, the antecedent of saḥ/asau/ayam is left deictic rather than identified doctrinally from the commentary.
Grammar alone does not force the technical rendering 'Dharma-body'; therefore dharmākhyaḥ is rendered more literally as 'called Dharma'.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 reports compound splits such as vimukti_Cp + kāyaḥ, dharma_Cp + ākhyaḥ, and mahā_Cp + muneḥ; per the instructions, the lemma/stem of an inflected compound is reported as the whole compound stem: vimuktikāya, dharmākhya, and mahāmuni.

For dharmākhyaḥ, ByT5 gives the final member as ākhyā_SNM. I analyze the inflected word as the masculine nominative singular compound stem dharmākhya, with -ākhya functioning adjectivally as 'called/named', while its semantic vigraha uses ākhyā 'appellation'.
open_questions: Without commentary, the exact doctrinal antecedent of 'that very one' cannot be fixed from this verse alone.
Without commentary, dhātu could be translated more specifically as 'realm', 'element', or 'constituent' depending on doctrinal context.
Without commentary, the relation between 'body of liberation' and 'called Dharma' remains grammatical apposition rather than a fully explained technical identification.
